title: Convert units multi-step word problems (metric)
description: Solve word problems that involve converting between metric measures of distance, volume, and mass, as well as measures of time.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cory made $4{,}500 \text{ g}$ of candy.  He saved $1\text{ kg}$ to eat later.  He divided the rest of the candy over $7$ bowls to serve at his party.  

**How  many *grams* of  candy did Cory serve in each bowl?**   

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{g}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert the $1\text { kg}$ Cory saved to grams.  

$1 \text{ kg}=\purpleD{1{,}000}\text{ g}$

Cory made $4{,}500 \text{ g}$ and saved $\purpleD{1{,}000}\text{ g}$.

We subtract to find out how much candy was left to serve at Cory's party.

$4{,}500-\purpleD{1{,}000}=\blueE{3{,}500}\text{ g}$

Cory has $\goldE{3{,}500}\text{ g}$ of candy to serve at the party.  

He divides the $\goldE{3{,}500}\text{ g}$ evenly between $\maroonD7$ bowls.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\goldE{3{,}500}\div\maroonD7 = \tealE{500} \text{ g}$
Cory served $\tealE{500}\text{ g}$ in each bowl.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
James has $14{,}500\text { g}$ of sand in his sandbox.  He brings home another $7{,}400\text { g}$ of sand from the beach to add to his sandbox.  
 
**How  many *kilograms* of  sand does James have in his sandbox now?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{kg}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
James had $14{,}500\text { g}$ and added  $7{,}400\text { g}$ more.  


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\goldE{14{,}500}\\
\underline{+~~\maroonD{7{,}400}}\\
\blueE{21{,}900}\end{align}$


James now has $\blueE{21{,}900}\text { g}$ of sand in his sandbox.

We need to convert $\blueE{21{,}900}\text { g}$ to kilograms.  

$1 \text{ g}=\dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ kg}$

$\begin {align}
&\quad{21{,}900} \text{ g}\\\\
&= {{21{,}900} \text{ g}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{{1{,}000 \text{ g}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{21{,}900 \cancel{\text{ g}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ g}}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{21{,}900 \text{ kg}}}{{1{,}000}} \\\\
&= {21{,}900 \text{ kg}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&={21.9\text{ kg}}\end{align}$
James has $21.9\text { kg}$ of sand in his sandbox.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Jemma filled one-fourth of a barrel with compost on Saturday.  Then she filled the remaining space with $4{,}200\text{ g}$ of compost on Sunday.  

**How many *kilograms* of compost are in the barrel?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilograms


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
After using up one fourth of the space in the barrel on Saturday, Jemma filled  the remaining $\dfrac{3}{4}$ on Sunday.  


[[☃ image 1]]

We need to split $\blueE{4{,}200}$ grams into $3$ equal parts.  

$\blueE{4{,}200}\div3=1{,}400\text{ g}$

So each fourth of the barrel contains $1{,}400\text{ g}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

How many grams are in the entire barrel?
The entire barrel contains $1{,}400 \times 4 = 5{,}600\text{ g}$.  


[[☃ image 1]]

We need to convert $5{,}600\text{ g}$ to kilograms. 
$1 \text{ g}=\dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ kg}$

$\begin {align}
&\quad{5{,}600} \text{ g}\\\\
&= {{5{,}600} \text{ g}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{{1{,}000 \text{ g}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{5{,}600 \cancel{\text{ g}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ g}}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{5{,}600 \text{ kg}}}{{1{,}000}} \\\\
&= {5{,}600 \text{ kg}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&={5.6\text{ kg}}\end{align}$

There are $5.6\text { kilograms}$ of compost in the barrel.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
A Girl Scout camp served half of their granola with breakfast.  After dinner, they put the remaining $6{,}250 \text{ g}$ of granola on top of their yogurt dessert.    

**How many *kilograms* of granola did the Girl Scout camp start with?**   

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilograms


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Half of the granola is $6{,}250 \text { g}$.  So, we multiply by $2$ to find the total amount of granola. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$6{,}250 \times 2 = 12{,}500 \text { g}$

The Girl Scout camp started with $12{,}500 \text { g}$ of granola.


[[☃ image 1]]

We need to convert $12{,}500 \text { g}$ to kilograms.
The Girl Scout camp started with $12.5\text { kilograms}$ of granola.

$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|

assessment_items.question_content:
Noah made $12 \text{ kg}$ of trail mix for his family's hiking trip.  His family ate $8{,}600 \text{ g}$ of the trail mix on the hiking trip.

**How  many *grams* of  trail mix did Noah have left?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{g}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $12\text { kg}$ to grams.  

$1 \text{ kg}=1{,}000\text{ g}$

So, $12 \text{ kg}=12\times1{,}000=12{,}000\text{ g}$.

We can subtract to see how much trail mix Noah has left.  



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\purpleD{12{,}000}\\
\underline{-~~\maroonD{8{,}600}}\\
\blueE{3{,}400}\end{align}$
Noah had $3{,}400 \text{ g}$ of trail mix left.

- | | | |

$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|

assessment_items.question_content:
Cora sliced $18 \text{ kg}$ of apples for a party.  She divided the apple slices equally between $5$ large bowls.
 
**How  many *grams* of  apple slices did Cora put in each bowl?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{g}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $18\text { kg}$ to grams.  

$1 \text{ kg}=1{,}000\text{ g}$

So, $18 \text{ kg}=18\times1{,}000=18{,}000\text{ g}$.
[[☃ image 1]]

Cora divided $\purpleD{18{,}000} \text{ g}$ into $\tealE5$ equal parts.


$\purpleD{18{,}000}\div \tealE5= \maroonD{3{,}600}\text{ g}$

Cora put $3{,}600 \text{ g}$ of apple slices in each bowl.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kevin started his science test at  $2{:}34\text { p.m.}$ and finished the test $75$ minutes later. Kevin has football practice at $4{:}15 \text{ p.m.}$  

**How much time did Kevin have between the end of the test and the beginning of football practice?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
&2{:}34 + \tealE{1\text{ hour}}=3{:}34\\\\
&3{:}34 +\purpleD{15\text{ minutes}}=3{:}49
\end{align}$

Kevin finished his test at $3{:}49\text{ p.m.}$

How much time did Kevin have between $3{:}49\text{ p.m.}$ and the beginning of football practice at $4{:}15\text{ p.m.}?$ 



[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{11}\text{ mins} + \maroonD{15}\text{ mins} = 26\text{ mins}$
Kevin had ${26}$ minutes between the end of the test and the beginning of football practice.

assessment_items.question_content:
Abigail's mom said she will be home from work in $65$ minutes. It is $4{:}27\text{ p.m.}$, and Abigail has dance practice at $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}$  

**How much time will Abigail have between the time that her mom gets home from work and the beginning of dance practice?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes



[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
&4{:}27 + \tealE{1\text{ hour}}=5{:}27\\\\
&5{:}27 +\purpleD{5\text{ minutes}}=5{:}32
\end{align}$

Abigail's mom will be home at $5{:}32\text{ p.m.}$

How much time will Abigail have between $5{:}32\text{ p.m.}$ and the beginning of dance practice at $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}?$



[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{13}\text{ mins} + \maroonD{15}\text{ mins} = 28\text{ mins}$
Abigail will have $28$ minutes between the time that her mom gets home from work and the beginning of dance practice.

assessment_items.question_content:
Susan started her homework at  $1{:}59\text { p.m.}$ and finished her homework $96$ minutes later.  Susan had volleyball practice at $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$ 

**How much time did Susan have between finishing her homework and the beginning of volleyball practice?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
&1{:}59 + \tealE{1\text{ hour}}=2{:}59\\\\
&2{:}59 +\purpleD{36\text{ minutes}}=3{:}35
\end{align}$

Susan finished her homework at $3{:}35\text{ p.m.}$

How much time did Susan have between $3{:}35\text{ p.m.}$ and the beginning of volleyball practice at $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}?$


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{10}\text{ mins} + \maroonD{15}\text{ mins} = 25\text{ mins}$
Susan had ${25}$ minutes between finishing her homework and the beginning of volleyball practice.

How much time will Emma have between $5{:}22\text{ p.m.}$ and the beginning of gymnastics lessons at $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}?$

Payton leaves to go on an $85$ minute bike ride at $3{:}27\text{ p.m.}$  Payton's family eats dinner at $5{:}45\text{ p.m.}$ 

**How much time will Payton have between finishing her bike ride and eating dinner?**

&4{:}27 +\purpleD{25\text{ minutes}}=4{:}52
\end{align}$

Payton finishes her bike ride at $4{:}52\text{ p.m.}$

How much time will Payton have between $4{:}52\text{ p.m.}$ and dinner $5{:}45\text{ p.m.}?$


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{8}\text{ mins} + \maroonD{45}\text{ mins} = 53\text{ mins}$
Payton will have ${53}$ minutes between finishing her bike ride and eating dinner.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tony started his math project at  $1{:}57\text { p.m.}$ and finished the project $80$ minutes later. Tony has band practice at $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$  

**How much time did Tony have between the end of the project and the beginning of band practice?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes


[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
&1{:}57 + \tealE{1\text{ hour}}=2{:}57\\\\
&2{:}57 +\purpleD{20\text{ minutes}}=3{:}17
\end{align}$

Tony finished his project at $3{:}17\text{ p.m.}$

How much time did Tony have between  $3{:}17\text{ p.m.}$ and the beginning of band practice at $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}?$



[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{13}\text{ mins} + \maroonD{30}\text{ mins} = 43\text{ mins}$
Tony had ${43}$ minutes between finishing his math project and the beginning of band practice.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gina mixed paint to make her favorite shade of purple. She filled one third of the container with red paint.  Then she filled the remaining space with $4\text{ L}$ of blue paint.  

**How many *milliliters* of  paint did Gina mix?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] milliliters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
After using one third of the space in the container for red paint, Gina filled  the remaining $\dfrac{2}{3}$ with blue paint.  



[[☃ image 1]]

We can split $4$ liters into $2$ groups of $2\text{ L}$.

So each third of Gina's paint mix contains $2\text{ L}$ of paint.


[[☃ image 1]]


How many liters was the whole paint mix?
The whole paint mix was $2 \times 3 = 6\text{ L}$.  


[[☃ image 1]]



We need to convert $6$ liters to milliliters. 
$1 \text{ L}=1{,}000\text{ mL}$

So, $6 \text{ L}=6\times 1{,}000=6{,}000\text{ mL}$.

Gina mixed $6{,}000$ milliliters of paint.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Coach Kelly brought $32 \text{ L}$ of water to the football game, and she divided the water equally between $8$ coolers.  

**How  many *milliliters* of  water did Coach Kelly put in each cooler?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{mL}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Coach divided $32 \text{ L}$ into $8$ equal parts.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleD{32\text{ L }} \div \tealE 8 = \maroonD{4 \text{ L}}$

Each cooler contained $\maroonD{4 \text{ L}}$ of water.

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD{4\text{ L}}$ to milliliters.

$1 \text{ L} = 1{,}000\text{ mL}$.

So, $\maroonD{4\text{ L}}=4 \times {1{,}000} = 4{,}000 \text{ mL}$.
Coach Kelly put $4{,}000 \text{ mL}$ of water in each cooler.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Tony made $14 \text{ L}$ of lemonade for a party.  His guests drank $9{,}500 \text{ mL}$ of the lemonade.

**How  many *milliliters* of  lemonade did Tony have left over?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{mL}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $14\text { L}$ to milliliters.  

$1 \text{ L}=1{,}000\text{ mL}$

So, $14 \text{ L}=14\times 1{,}000=14{,}000\text{ mL}$.
Tony made $14{,}000\text{ mL}$ of lemonade, and his guests drank $9{,}500\text{ mL}$.  



[[☃ image 1]]

We can subtract to find out how much lemonade Tony had left over.  

$\begin{align}14{,}000\\
\underline{-~~9{,}500}\\
4{,}500\end{align}$



[[☃ image 1]]

Tony had $4{,}500 \text { mL}$ of lemonade left over.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Wes mixed different juices to make fruit punch. He filled one-half of the punch bowl with orange juice.  Then he filled half of the remaining space with grape juice.  Finally, he filled the rest of the punch bowl with $300\text{ mL}$ of cherry juice.  

**How many *liters* of fruit punch did Wes make?**  


[[☃ input-number 1]] liters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
After filling half of the space in the container with orange juice, Wes filled half of the remaining space with grape juice and the other half with $\maroonD{300} \text { mL}$ of cherry juice.  


[[☃ image 1]]

So, there is also $\purpleD{300} \text{ mL}$ of grape juice.

We need to add the amount of grape juice and cherry juice to determine the amount of orange juice.

$\maroonD{300} + \purpleD{300} = \goldE{600 \text{ mL}}$


[[☃ image 1]]

How many milliliters are in the whole fruit punch mix?

The whole fruit punch mix was $\goldE{600} + \purpleD{300} + \maroonD{300}= \tealE{1{,}200\text{ mL}}$.  


[[☃ image 1]]

We need to convert $1{,}200$ milliliters to liters.
Wes mixed $1.2$ liters of fruit punch.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Molly made $3{,}600 \text{ mL}$ of tea for a party, and she served the tea divided equally in $12$ cups.  

**How many *liters* of tea did Molly put in each cup?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{L}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Molly divided $3{,}600 \text{ mL}$ into $12$ equal parts.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{3{,}600 \text{ mL }} \div \tealE {12} = \maroonD{300 \text{ mL}}$

We need to convert $300 \text{ mL}$ to liters.

$1 \text{ mL}=\dfrac{1}{1{,}000}$ of a liter

$\begin {align}
&\quad{300} \text{ mL}\\\\
&= {{300} \text{ mL}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \text{ mL}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{300 \cancel{\text{ mL}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ mL}}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{300 \text{ L}}}{{1{,}000}} \\\\
&= {300 \text{ L}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&={0.3\text{ L}}\end{align}$
Molly put $0.3\text{ L}$ of tea in each glass.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeter| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Thomas made $8{,}700 \text{ mL}$ of tomato soup.  He packed $1.6 \text{ L}$ of the soup in his kids' lunches.  He then froze half of the remaining soup.

**How  many *milliliters* of soup did Thomas freeze?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{mL}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $1.6\text{ L}$ to milliliters.  

$1 \text{ L}=1{,}000\text{ mL}$

So, $1.6 \text{ L}=1.6\times 1{,}000=1{,}600\text{ mL}$.

Thomas packed $1{,}600 \text { mL}$ of soup in his kids' lunches.  


[[☃ image 1]]

We can subtract to find out how much he has left. 

$\begin{align}\tealE{8{,}700}\\
\underline{-~~\goldE{1{,}600}}\\
7{,}100\end{align}$

He has $7{,}100 \text{ mL}$ of soup after packing his kids' lunches.  

He froze half of this amount.  What is half of $7{,}100 \text{ mL}?$

To find half of $7{,}100 \text{ mL}$, we divide by $2$. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$7{,}100 \div2= 3{,}550 \text{ mL}$
Thomas froze $3{,}550 \text{ mL}$ of soup.

assessment_items.question_content:
On Martin's first stroke, his golf ball traveled $\dfrac45$ of the distance to the hole.   On his second stroke, the ball traveled $79$ meters and went into the hole.  

**How  many *kilometers* from the hole was Martin when he started?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Martin's ball had to travel $\dfrac15$ of the total distance when he hit his second shot.    

His second shot traveled $79 \text{ m}$.  So, $\dfrac15$ of the distance to the hole is $79 \text { m}$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

We want to know what distance all $\dfrac55$ represent, so we multiply $79 \times 5$. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$79 \times 5 = 395$  

The total distance to the hole was $395$  meters.

$395 \text{ m} = \,? \text{ km}$

$1 \text{ m}=\dfrac{1}{1{,}000}\text { km}$

$\begin {align}
&\quad{395} \text{ m}\\\\
&= {{395} \text{ m}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ km}}{{1{,}000 \text{ m}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{395 \cancel{\text{ m}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ km}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ m}}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{395 \text{ km}}}{{1{,}000}} \\\\
&= {395 \text{ km}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&={0.395\text{ km}}\end{align}$
Martin was $0.395$ kilometers from the hole when he started.

assessment_items.question_content:
A bee flew $840 \text{ cm}$ and landed on a flower to collect some pollen.  Then the bee flew another $330 \text { cm}$ to get back to her hive.

**How  many *meters* did the bee travel?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters



[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
The bee traveled  $840 \text{ cm}$ to gather the pollen and  $330 \text{ cm}$ after.  We add to find her total distance.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\goldE{840}\\
\underline{+~~\purpleD{330}}\\
1{,}170\end{align}$

The bee traveled $1{,}170 \text{ cm}$.

We need to convert $1{,}170 \text{ cm}$ to meters.

$1 \text{ cm}=\dfrac{1}{100}\text { m}$

$\begin {align}
&\quad{1{,}170} \text{ cm}\\\\
&= {{1{,}170} \text{ cm}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ m}}{{100 \text{ cm}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{1{,}170 \cancel{\text{ cm}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ m}}{{100 \cancel{\text{ cm}}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{1{,}170 \text{ m}}}{{100}} \\\\
&= {1{,}170 \text{ m}}\div{100}\\\\
&={11.7\text{ m}}\end{align}$
The bee traveled $11.7$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
A carpenter had a board that was $5.28 \text { m}$ long.  She used her circular saw to cut $9 \text{ cm}$ off the end of the board.

**How  many *centimeters* long was the board after the carpenter cut it?**    

[[☃ input-number 1]] centimeters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $5.28 \text{ m}$ to centimeters.

$1\text{ m} = 100 \text { cm}$ 

So, $5.28\text{ m} = 5.28 \times {100}=528\text{ cm}$.
The carpenter cut $9 \text{ cm}$ off the end of the board.   We subtract to find the new length of the board.

$\begin{align}528\\
\underline{- ~~~9}\\
519\end{align}$

The board was $519 \text{ centimeters}$ long after the carpenter cut it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Issa jogged two-thirds of the way home from school.  Then he was tired, so he walked the remaining $3{,}200\text{ m}$.

**How  many *kilometers* did Issa travel from school to his house?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Issa had $\dfrac13$ more of the way to go when he started to walk.    

Issa walked $\purpleD{3{,}200 \text{ m}}$.  So, $\dfrac13$ of the way to his house is $3{,}200 \text{ m}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

We want to know what distance all $\dfrac33$ represent, so we multiply $\purpleD{3{,}200} \times 3$.

$3{,}200 \times 3 = 9{,}600 \text{ m}$  



[[☃ image 1]]

The total distance Issa traveled was $9{,}600$ meters.

$9{,}600 \text{ m} = \,? \text{ km}$
Issa traveled $9.6$ kilometers from school to his house.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lisa went on a $52 \text { km}$ hike.  She divided the distance traveled evenly over $4$ days.  

**How  many *meters* did Lisa walk each day?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Lisa divided $52 \text{ km}$ into $4$ equal parts.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleD{52}\text{ km } \div \tealE 4 = \maroonD{13} \text{ km}$

Lisa walked $\maroonD{13} \text{ km}$ each day.

We need to convert $13 \text{ km}$ to meters.

$1\text{ km} = 1{,}000 \text { m}$ 

So, $13\text{ km} = 13 \times {1{,}000}=13{,}000\text{ m}$.
Lisa walked $13{,}000 \text{ meters}$ each day.

assessment_items.question_content:
The library is $14.2 \text { km}$ away from Daniel's apartment.  The bus takes Daniel $11.15 \text { km}$ of the way to the library.  He has to walk the remaining distance.  

**How  many *meters* does Daniel have to walk?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
We subtract the distance the bus takes Daniel from the total distance to the library to find out how far Daniel has to walk.  

$\begin{align}14.20\\
\underline{-~~11.15}\\
3.05\end{align}$

Daniel walks $3.05 \text{ km}$.

We need to convert $3.05 \text{ km}$ to meters.

$1\text{ km} = 1{,}000 \text { m}$ 

So, $3.05\text{ km} = 3.05 \times {1{,}000}=3{,}050\text{ m}$.
Daniel walks $3{,}050 \text{ meters}$.
